definition: Age-related loss of muscle mass and strength.
High blood pressure that increases risk of heart disease.
A sudden change in mental status, often acute and reversible.

The cell’s self-cleaning process that recycles damaged components.
A blood test measuring average glucose levels over the past 2–3 months.
The brain's sleep-driven waste clearance system.
The fraction of a dose that reaches the bloodstream unchanged.

A degenerative joint condition caused by cartilage breakdown.
An autoimmune disorder that causes joint inflammation.
A condition where bones become weak and brittle.
A measure of bone strength.
A group of conditions affecting memory, thinking, and behavior.

A decrease in memory, attention, or decision-making abilities.

A sudden change in mental status.

A transient ischemic attack, often called a mini-stroke.
A condition where the heart cannot pump blood effectively.
An irregular heartbeat pattern.
A common arrhythmia that increases stroke risk.
A chronic lung disease that makes breathing difficult.
A lung condition where air sacs are damaged.
Long-term inflammation of the airways.
A condition of reduced strength and resilience in older adults.
The likelihood of experiencing a fall due to balance or mobility issues.
Nerve damage causing numbness or tingling in the limbs.
Loss of bladder or bowel control.
A urinary tract infection affecting the bladder or kidneys.
An eye condition that can damage the optic nerve.
Clouding of the eye’s lens that affects vision.
A condition affecting central vision.
Reduced ability to hear sounds clearly.
Ringing or buzzing in the ears.
Long-lasting pain that persists beyond normal healing.
The body's response to injury or infection.
The body's reaction to harmful stimuli.
A condition where the immune system attacks the body.
The body's defense system against illness.
A microorganism that can cause disease.
A tiny infectious agent that replicates inside living cells.
Single-celled organisms that can be helpful or harmful.
A type of organism that includes molds and yeasts.
A protein produced by the immune system to fight infections.
A substance that triggers an immune response.
Muscle pain or soreness, often widespread during viral infections.
Inflammation of the smallest air passages in the lungs, common in RSV cases.
A laboratory technique that can detect multiple viruses from a single sample simultaneously.
Laboratory-made proteins that mimic the immune system’s ability to fight harmful pathogens.
Protection against disease provided by antibodies produced outside the body.
A class of drugs that block the enzyme viruses use to spread.
A condition caused by insufficient fluid intake.
Lack of proper nutrients for health.
A condition where the body lacks enough healthy red blood cells.
A carbohydrate made of two linked sugar molecules.
A five‑carbon sugar alcohol used as a low-calorie sweetener.
A sugar alcohol commonly used as a sweetener that does not raise blood glucose significantly.
A measure of how slowly or quickly a food raises blood glucose.
The overall balance of acidity and alkalinity in the mouth.
Damage to teeth caused by acids produced by bacteria.
Advantages to oral health such as reduced cavity risk or improved enamel strength.
A mineral important for tooth structure and enamel strength.
A common oral bacterium associated with cavity formation.
The natural process of restoring minerals to tooth enamel.
Fluid produced in the mouth that protects teeth and supports digestion.
A sticky film of bacteria that forms on teeth.
The overall condition of teeth, gums, and oral tissues.
The hard outer surface of the tooth that protects against decay.
A condition where bones become weaker and more prone to fracture.
Cells that break down old bone tissue as part of natural bone remodeling.
The rigid structures that support the body and protect organs.
A mineral essential for bone strength and many body functions.
The condition of the digestive system and its influence on overall well-being.
Long-term health and lifespan influenced by genetics, lifestyle, and environment.
The study of genes and how they influence health and traits.
Actions that support better health and reduce future risk.
An approach focused on reducing disease risk before it develops.
Tools and innovations that enhance modern health practices.
Insights gained from clinical observation or research that help guide health decisions.
The ability to form new ideas or concepts.
Microorganisms that can live in the mouth and influence oral health.
Damage to teeth caused by acids produced by bacteria.
Damage to teeth caused by acids produced by bacteria.
The body's process of converting food into energy.
Conditions affecting thyroid hormone production.
Low thyroid hormone levels.
High thyroid hormone levels.
A fatty substance in the blood that can affect heart health.
A type of fat found in the blood.
The amount of sugar in the bloodstream.
A condition where the body struggles to regulate blood sugar.
A condition where cells do not respond well to insulin.
Emotional and physical strain experienced by caregivers.
A legal document outlining healthcare wishes.
A condition with sudden onset and short duration.
A long-lasting or persistent condition.
A procedure where a small tissue sample is examined.
The process of identifying a medical condition.
The expected outcome of a condition.
A physical or mental sign of a condition.
A trait or behavior that increases disease likelihood.
Actions taken to manage or improve a condition.
A treatment method for physical or mental health.
A chemical messenger that regulates body functions.
The study of inherited traits and DNA.
A cancer that begins in gland-forming tissues found throughout the body.
Cancer that develops in the bile ducts.
Yellowing of the skin and eyes caused by a buildup of bilirubin.
Surgical removal of part of an organ or tissue.
Treatments used alongside standard medical care to support well-being.
it is the fraction or percentage of an administered dose that reaches the bloodstream unchanged.

An imbalance where harmful, unstable molecules called free radicals (Reactive Oxygen Species - ROS) overwhelm the body's antioxidant defenses, causing damage to cells, proteins, and DNA, contributing to aging and chronic diseases like cancer, diabetes, and heart disease.
Cap the ends of chromosomes, acting like plastic tips on shoelaces to prevent them from fraying, sticking together, or being recognized as damaged DNA.
a vital steroid hormone from your adrenal glands that manages stress, metabolism (blood sugar, fats, proteins), blood pressure, and inflammation
The 'powerhouses' of the cell that convert nutrients into ATP (energy) to fuel all bodily functions.
The brain’s lifelong ability to reorganize itself by forming new neural connections in response to learning or recovery from injury.
The community of microorganisms living in the gut that regulates digestion, immunity, and metabolic health.
A state of persistent, low-grade chronic inflammation that develops with age and drives many age-related diseases.
Unstable molecules that can damage cell structures and DNA, a process known as oxidative stress.
The brain's ability to find alternative ways to function and maintain mental agility despite damage or age-related changes.
Beneficial plant compounds with antioxidant properties that protect cells from damage and support heart and brain health.
A measurement of the amount of minerals (like calcium) in bone tissue, used to determine bone strength and fracture risk.
The body's 'self-cleaning' process where cells break down and recycle their own damaged components to maintain health.
Small proteins released by cells that act as chemical messengers to coordinate the body's immune response and inflammation.
The internal 24-hour clock that regulates sleep, hormone release, and metabolism based on light and darkness.
The state of steady internal physical and chemical balance maintained by living systems for optimal survival.
Sticky protein buildups that collect between nerve cells in the brain, often associated with the progression of Alzheimer’s disease.
Protective DNA caps at chromosome ends that shorten with each cell division, impacting cellular aging.
An imbalance between free radicals and antioxidants that can damage cells and accelerate aging.
Affecting the entire body or multiple organ systems, often involving widespread immune or inflammatory responses.
Conditions involving progressive loss of neurons, impairing memory, movement, or cognition.
Relating to the hippocampus, a memory‑forming brain region vulnerable to stress and aging.
A brain structure that processes fear, threat, and emotional significance, shaping stress responses.
A condition where a person has two sets of DNA.
A condition where a person has two sets of DNA.
Presence of a small number of cells from another individual.
Presence of a small number of cells from another individual.
A sleep-driven system that clears waste from the brain.
A sleep-driven system that clears waste from the brain.
The community of microorganisms living in the human gut.
The community of microorganisms living in the human gut.
A beneficial response to an inactive treatment due to expectation.
A beneficial response to an inactive treatment due to expectation.
Natural pain-relieving chemicals produced by the body.
Natural pain-relieving chemicals produced by the body.
The continuous cycle of bone breakdown and rebuilding.
The continuous cycle of bone breakdown and rebuilding.
Changes in gene expression without altering DNA sequence.
Changes in gene expression without altering DNA sequence.
Electrical signals that guide healing and tissue repair.
Electrical signals that guide healing and tissue repair.
Research exploring whether humans can enter a low-metabolic torpor state.
Research exploring whether humans can enter a low-metabolic torpor state.
A marker that helps show how much insulin your body is producing.
A wearable device that tracks glucose levels throughout the day and night.
A natural early-morning rise in blood sugar caused by hormone changes.
Your blood sugar level after not eating for at least eight hours.
Slow stomach emptying that can cause nausea, bloating, or early fullness.
A type of diabetes medicine that helps control appetite and blood sugar.
High blood sugar that may cause thirst, fatigue, or frequent urination.
Low blood sugar that may cause shakiness, sweating, or confusion.
When the body has trouble using insulin effectively, causing higher glucose levels.
Chemicals made when the body burns fat for energy; high levels can be dangerous.
A common first-line diabetes medication that helps reduce glucose production in the liver.
Nerve damage that can cause tingling, numbness, or burning sensations.
Your blood sugar level after eating a meal.
A medication that helps the kidneys remove extra glucose through urine.
Naturally occurring compounds in plants that can bind to minerals such as calcium.
Naturally occurring compounds in plants that can bind to minerals such as calcium.
A type of kidney stone formed when calcium binds with oxalate.
A type of kidney stone formed when calcium binds with oxalate.
A gut bacterium that helps break down oxalates in the digestive tract.
Foods that contain higher levels of oxalates, such as spinach, almonds, and beets.
A dietary pattern that limits foods high in oxalates.
The process by which oxalates are absorbed through the digestive tract.
A term sometimes used to describe symptoms people attribute to changes in oxalate intake.
A term sometimes used to describe symptoms people attribute to changes in oxalate intake.
Movement that increases heart rate and breathing, such as walking or swimming. Supports heart, lung, and brain health.
Exercises that build muscle and bone strength using resistance bands, weights, or bodyweight. Helps prevent falls.
Exercises that improve stability and coordination, reducing fall risk. Includes tai chi and heel-to-toe walking.
Stretching movements that improve range of motion and reduce stiffness.
Movement that reduces stress on joints, such as swimming, cycling, or chair aerobics.
Seated or supported aerobic movements designed for seniors with limited mobility.
Exercises that mimic everyday movements like standing, lifting, or reaching to improve independence.
Activity that raises heart rate but still allows conversation, such as brisk walking.
High-effort activity that significantly increases heart rate and breathing.
Alternating short bursts of activity with rest or slower movement.
Activities that build stamina and cardiovascular health over time.
Movements that improve joint comfort and ease of motion.
How far a joint can move in each direction. Often decreases with age.
The pattern of how a person walks, including speed, stride, and balance.
The alignment of the body while sitting or standing. Affects balance and comfort.
Strength of the muscles in the abdomen, back, and pelvis that support stability.
The ability of a joint to move freely and comfortably.
The ability to move safely and independently during daily tasks.
The ability to stay steady while standing still.
The ability to stay steady while moving.
The ability to move different parts of the body smoothly and efficiently.
The ability to maintain control of body position during movement.
The likelihood of losing balance or falling. Increased by weak muscles, medications, or vision changes.
Gentle movements performed before exercise to prepare muscles and reduce injury risk.
Light movement and stretching after exercise to help the body recover.
Pushing the body beyond safe limits, causing dizziness, pain, or extreme fatigue.
Stress or irritation in a joint caused by overuse or improper movement.
Using correct body alignment during exercise to prevent injury.
Maintaining adequate fluid intake before, during, and after exercise.
A warning sign from the body indicating that an exercise may be unsafe or too intense.
A feeling of tiredness that may indicate the need for rest or lighter activity.
Difficulty breathing during activity; may signal overexertion or a medical issue.
A stretchy band used for strength training. Gentle on joints and ideal for seniors.
Handheld weights used for strength training. Light weights are ideal for beginners.
A large inflatable ball used for core and balance exercises.
A cushioned mat used for stretching, yoga, and floor exercises.
A tool used to massage muscles and improve flexibility.
Poles used to improve stability and reduce joint impact during walking.
Light weights worn around the ankles to increase resistance during leg exercises.
A sturdy chair used for balance, seated exercise, or modified strength training.
A machine used for indoor walking or light jogging.
A low-impact cardio machine that reduces stress on joints.
Naturally occurring compounds in plants that can bind to minerals such as calcium.
A diet designed to reduce oxalate intake to help prevent kidney stone formation.
A helpful gut bacterium that breaks down oxalates and may reduce kidney stone risk.
A protein found in wheat, barley, and rye that can trigger immune reactions in susceptible individuals.
Finger‑like projections in the small intestine that absorb nutrients.
A condition where the gut lining becomes more porous, allowing particles to pass into the bloodstream.
A condition where gluten causes symptoms without autoimmune markers.
Fermentable carbohydrates that can mimic gluten symptoms.
An enzyme involved in celiac autoimmune reactions.
an overwhelming and irrational dread of doctors, medical settings, or procedures.
A condition where blood sugar is higher than normal but not yet in the diabetes range.
When the body’s cells do not respond well to insulin, causing blood sugar to rise.
Blood sugar measured after not eating for at least 8 hours.
Dark, velvety skin patches often linked to insulin resistance.
The release of gas from the digestive tract
The process where gut bacteria break down food, producing gas..
A synthetic sugar used to help remove ammonia in liver disease.
A waste product that can build up when the liver is damaged.
Molecules responsible for the smell in some types of gas.
A technology that delivers genetic instructions to cells to trigger an immune response.
A waste product that can build up when the liver is damaged.
Transplanting organs from one species to another.
Protein deposits associated with Alzheimer’s disease.
Computer systems that analyze medical data to assist clinicians.
3D printing of biological tissues using living cells.
Total greenhouse gases caused by an individual or household.
Diet choices that support health and reduce emissions
3D printing of biological tissues using living cells.
Total greenhouse gases caused by an individual or household.
Diet choices that support health and reduce emissions
Using less energy for the same service.
Protecting natural systems for long‑term health.
Habits that increase connection with nature.
Replacing products with safer alternatives.
Maintaining physical, mental, and social well‑being as you grow older.
Exercises that strengthen muscles using weights or body resistance.
Brain skills used for planning, focus, and decision‑making.
Maintaining physical, mental, and social well‑being as you grow older.
Exercises that improve stability and prevent falls.
Actions taken to avoid illness rather than treat it later.
Gradual changes in DNA methylation patterns that alter gene activity and contribute to aging
A decline in mitochondrial efficiency that reduces cellular energy production and accelerates aging.
The cell’s recycling process that removes damaged proteins and debris to maintain healthy function.
Chronic, low‑grade inflammation that increases with age and contributes to many age‑related diseases.
A measure of cellular aging based on biomarkers, often differing from chronological age.
Hormonal and inflammatory signals from the hypothalamus that influence metabolism, stress responses, and aging.
The monthly amount you pay for Medicare coverage.
The amount you pay out of pocket before Medicare begins covering services.
A percentage of costs you pay after meeting your deductible.
A list of prescription drugs covered by a Medicare Part D plan.
The group of doctors and facilities contracted with a Medicare Advantage plan.
Supplemental insurance that helps cover costs not paid by Original Medicare.
The network of glands that produce hormones regulating metabolism, growth, and stress response.
A condition where hormone levels are too high or too low, affecting energy, weight, and mood.
Conditions affecting how the body converts food into energy, such as diabetes or lipid disorders.
The function of the thyroid gland in regulating metabolism, energy, and body temperature.
Glands that produce cortisol and adrenaline to regulate stress, blood pressure, and metabolism.
A protein that transports glucose into muscle and fat cells in response to insulin or exercise.
Neurochemicals that alleviate pain and promote feelings of well-being during physical activity.
A protein that forms abnormal aggregates in the brain, common in Lewy Body Dementia.
A severe adverse reaction to antipsychotic medications often seen in patients with LBD.
Spontaneous and rapid changes in alertness, attention, or thinking ability.
A sleep disorder where individuals physically act out vivid or unpleasant dreams.
Vitamins A, D, E, and K that require dietary fat for absorption and storage.
Vitamins B and C that dissolve in water and require regular intake as they are not stored.
The process of nutrients moving from the digestive tract into the bloodstream.
A clinically low nutrient level that may impair health or cause specific symptoms.
A supplement containing various vitamins and minerals to support general health.
A product intended to add nutritional value to the diet when food intake is insufficient.
An AI system developed by Google DeepMind to predict complex protein structures.
AI models used to generate text, images, or data summaries to assist clinical workflows.
A plausible-sounding but incorrect or fabricated output generated by an AI model.
Integrated data types, such as genomics and imaging, used for comprehensive health insights.
The period of life spent in good health, free from chronic disease or disability.
The fair opportunity for every individual to attain their full health potential.
Non-medical factors like housing and food security that influence health outcomes.
Medical care tailored to an individual’s genetic, environmental, and lifestyle profile.
The presence of two or more chronic health conditions in a single individual.
Drugs that improve cognitive function by increasing acetylcholine levels in the brain.
The concurrent use of multiple medications, typically five or more, by one patient.
The cycling of individuals on and off Medicaid due to income or administrative shifts.
A profound transformation in form, function, or understanding. In aging, it often refers to the inner changes that occur as people adapt, grow, and reinterpret their life experiences.
A meaningful change in how a person views life, purpose, or values. Often emerges in later life as priorities evolve and individuals seek deeper meaning and clarity.
Deep wisdom gained through experience, reflection, and insight. Sagacity often strengthens with age as individuals integrate lessons learned across a lifetime.
The ability to understand and share the feelings of another person. Empathy supports healthy relationships, emotional well-being, and social connection throughout the lifespan.
A developmental theory describing a natural shift in older adulthood toward greater reflection, inner peace, and a broader, more spiritual or existential perspective.
The body’s return to a calm, restorative state after stress, driven by the parasympathetic nervous system. Supports heart health, digestion, emotional regulation, and resilience.
A brain-related complication of liver disease caused by toxins—especially ammonia—that the liver can no longer clear. Can lead to confusion, sleep changes, irritability, or slowed thinking.
A major blood protein made by the liver that helps maintain fluid balance, transport hormones, and support circulation. Low levels contribute to swelling and fatigue.
An intravenous infusion used to restore albumin levels, stabilize blood pressure, and protect kidney function during complications such as paracentesis or infection.
Enlarged veins in the esophagus caused by portal hypertension. These fragile veins can rupture and bleed, requiring monitoring and preventive treatment.
A procedure where rubber bands are placed around varices to stop bleeding or prevent future bleeding. Treats the veins, not the underlying portal pressure.
Medications that lower portal pressure and reduce the risk of variceal bleeding by slowing the heart rate and decreasing blood flow through the portal system.
A stage of cirrhosis where the liver is scarred but still performing enough essential functions to avoid major complications.
A stage of cirrhosis where the liver can no longer maintain normal function, leading to complications such as ascites, variceal bleeding, jaundice, or encephalopathy.

A hormone produced by the pancreas that allows glucose to enter cells for energy. Essential for blood sugar regulation. People with type 1 diabetes require insulin therapy; many with type 2 may also need it.
A simple sugar that serves as the body’s primary energy source. Blood glucose levels are tightly regulated by insulin and other hormones. Chronically high levels can damage organs and tissues.
A wearable device that continuously tracks glucose levels in the interstitial fluid. Helps detect trends, reduce hypoglycemia risk, and improve diabetes management without frequent fingersticks.
Damage to the retina’s blood vessels caused by diabetes. Can lead to vision loss or blindness. Regular eye exams are essential for early detection and treatment.
Kidney damage caused by diabetes. Early signs include protein in the urine. Progression can lead to chronic kidney disease or dialysis. Blood pressure and blood sugar control are key to prevention.
Macronutrients that include sugars, starches, and fiber. Carbs raise blood sugar and must be balanced with insulin or medications. Found in grains, fruits, vegetables, and sweets.
A ranking system that shows how quickly carbohydrate-containing foods raise blood sugar. Low-GI foods cause slower, more stable rises and are preferred for diabetes management.
A first-line oral medication for type 2 diabetes. Lowers blood sugar by reducing liver glucose production and improving insulin sensitivity. Also used in prediabetes and PCOS.
Medications that mimic the GLP-1 hormone to stimulate insulin release, suppress appetite, and slow digestion. Used in type 2 diabetes and obesity. Examples include semaglutide and liraglutide.
A condition where blood sugar drops below normal. Symptoms include shakiness, confusion, sweating, and fainting. Treated with fast-acting carbohydrates like juice or glucose tablets.
A condition where blood sugar is too high. Symptoms include thirst, fatigue, blurry vision, and frequent urination. Can lead to diabetic ketoacidosis or long-term organ damage.
Industrial food products made with additives, flavor enhancers, and minimal whole ingredients.
A heightened blood pressure response to dietary salt intake.
A chemical used in some can linings and plastics that may leach into food.
The labeled portion of food, which may represent only part of the package or can.
Water buildup in the body often triggered by high sodium intake.
An industry process where products are modified to reduce harmful nutrients or additives.
Advanced medications that target specific parts of the immune system.
A protein involved in inflammation, targeted by newer biologics.
Treatment using ultraviolet light.
A form of arthritis affecting some people with psoriasis.
Topical drugs that suppress immune activity.
Excessive cell growth, common in psoriasis.
A plant family (Solanaceae) that includes tomatoes, peppers, potatoes, and eggplant.
Natural defense chemicals in nightshades that may irritate sensitive individuals.
The botanical family that includes nightshade vegetables, fruits, and spices.
A compound in chili peppers responsible for heat; may irritate sensitive skin or digestion.
A nightshade fruit often marketed for antioxidants; may trigger symptoms in sensitive individuals.
Viruses engineered to deliver genetic material into cells.
Tiny lipid-based spheres used to encapsulate and deliver drugs or genetic material.
A technique that uses electrical pulses to temporarily open cell membranes for DNA or RNA entry.
Enzymes that cut or modify DNA as part of CRISPR gene-editing systems.
A gene-editing technology that uses guide RNA and Cas proteins to target specific DNA sequences.
The process of modifying an organism’s DNA to change or enhance traits.
Ultrafine particles used to transport drugs, RNA, or DNA into cells.
Methods used to transport therapeutic molecules into cells or tissues.
Direct injection of genetic material or tools into individual cells or embryos.
Patterns showing when and where genes are turned on or off in an organism.
The arrangement of exons, introns, and regulatory elements within a gene.
DNA sequences that control when, where, and how strongly genes are expressed.
Techniques that modify DNA or RNA to correct, remove, or replace genetic information.
An interdisciplinary field that designs and constructs new biological systems or reprograms existing ones.
Disorders caused by mutations or abnormalities in an individual's DNA.
A genotoxin produced by certain gut bacteria that can damage DNA and increase colon cancer risk.
A protein from Vibrio cholerae that selectively targets and kills colon cancer cells in early research.
Fragments of tumor DNA circulating in the bloodstream, used for early cancer detection and monitoring.
A hereditary condition caused by mutations in DNA mismatch repair genes, greatly increasing colon cancer risk.
A mutation in the PIK3CA gene that may predict strong response to low-dose aspirin therapy.
A minimally invasive test that detects tumor DNA in blood, often before tumors appear on scans.
A type of colon polyp with a higher likelihood of becoming cancerous if untreated.
A measure of how abnormal colon cells appear under a microscope, used to estimate cancer risk.
A hereditary condition causing hundreds of polyps in the colon, often requiring early preventive surgery.
A tumor marker sometimes elevated in colon cancer, used to monitor treatment response.
A type of colon cancer that develops from serrated polyps, often linked to specific genetic mutations.
A genetic test panel that checks for inherited mutations linked to colon cancer risk.
Programmed cell death — the built‑in 'self‑destruct' mechanism that MakA triggers in cancer cells.
A substance that is toxic to specific cells. In this context, a 'smart' toxin designed to selectively kill tumor cells.
The removal of a polyp during colonoscopy — a simple procedure that prevents cancer before it starts.
The total amount of inflammation affecting the body at one time. Smoking can increase this load, which may influence psoriasis symptoms.
When multiple small triggers—such as stress, smoking, or poor sleep—combine and make psoriasis flares more likely.
Reduced blood flow that can make skin appear dull or irritated. Smoking is known to contribute to this effect.
The redness, scaling, and discomfort associated with psoriasis, often influenced by inflammation and environmental triggers.
Psoriasis flare‑ups that occur or worsen during periods of emotional or physical stress.
A vegetables family including tomatoes, peppers, and eggplants that some people report sensitivity to.
Processed grains in white bread and pasta that may affect inflammation for some individuals.
Sugars added during processing that may influence inflammation in some people.
Oils such as soybean, corn, and sunflower oil that some people believe affect inflammation.
A reaction some people experience to milk or cheese that may affect joint symptoms.
Meats preserved with salt or additives that may influence inflammation.
Highly processed packaged foods that some people find worsen arthritis symptoms.
Low-calorie sweeteners that some individuals report sensitivity to.
The body's natural response to injury or irritation, which can affect joints.
Foods that may worsen symptoms for some individuals with arthritis.
Difficulty falling or staying asleep due to itching, pain, or emotional stress linked to psoriasis.
A pattern where systemic inflammation interferes with deep sleep, often seen in chronic conditions like psoriasis.
Increased itching during nighttime hours, commonly reported by people with psoriasis or eczema.
A feeling of skin discomfort or agitation that makes it hard to relax or sleep, often linked to inflammation.
Psoriasis flares that worsen after poor sleep or during periods of sleep disruption.
Advanced medications made from living cells that target specific parts of the immune system involved in psoriasis. Often used when topical treatments or light therapy are not enough.
A class of biologics that block tumor necrosis factor (TNF), a protein involved in inflammation. Commonly used for both psoriasis and psoriatic arthritis.
Biologic medications that target the IL‑17 pathway, which plays a role in plaque formation. Known for fast skin‑clearing effects in many patients.
Biologics that block the IL‑23 protein, an upstream driver of inflammation. Often associated with long-lasting results and fewer injections.
Oral medications that work inside immune cells to interrupt inflammatory signals. Sometimes used when injections are not preferred.
Medications designed to be highly similar to an existing biologic. They offer comparable effectiveness and may be used when insurance coverage changes.
Programs offered by drug manufacturers to help patients access medications when insurance coverage changes or costs become high.
Smooth, straight-line blood flow—like a quiet river. This is the goal of Stress Mastery and proper medication: keeping blood moving without creating the friction that leads to clots.
Disrupted, swirling blood flow caused by high pressure or vessel narrowing. This 'rough water' can damage vessel walls and is a primary risk factor during the 48-hour TIA window.
Increased blood pressure in the portal vein system—the liver’s plumbing. When this pressure is high, it can influence the entire systemic bridge, including brain circulation.
The narrowing of blood vessels. Stress and high cortisol cause this, 'kinking the hose' and forcing the heart to work harder, which spikes pressure in sensitive brain vessels.
The brain's ability to reorganize itself. Pillar 2 (Movement) encourages this, helping the brain stay resilient even if a TIA or minor vascular event occurs.
The Integrated Stress Response, a cellular safety mechanism that becomes overactive in Alzheimer’s, triggering toxic lipid production.
The balance of protein creation, folding, and clearance. Disrupted proteostasis contributes to neurodegeneration.
A protein that helps clear damaged mitochondria. Impaired Parkin activity is linked to Parkinson’s and Alzheimer’s.
A stress-induced microglial subtype associated with synapse loss, inflammation, and Alzheimer’s progression.
Cellular damage caused by misfolded or aggregated proteins, common in Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s.
A chronic inflammatory arthritis linked to psoriasis, involving joints, tendons, and the immune system.
A five‑question screening tool used to detect early signs of psoriatic arthritis in people with psoriasis.
An international consortium that develops evidence‑based treatment recommendations for psoriasis and psoriatic arthritis.
Diffuse swelling of an entire finger or toe caused by inflammation of joints, tendons, and soft tissues.
Inflammation where tendons or ligaments attach to bone, contributing to pain and stiffness in psoriatic arthritis.
A subtype of psoriatic arthritis affecting the spine and sacroiliac joints, causing inflammatory back pain and stiffness.
A chronic condition where stomach acid repeatedly flows back into the esophagus, causing irritation.
A burning sensation in the chest caused by acid moving upward into the esophagus.
A muscle ring at the bottom of the esophagus that prevents stomach acid from flowing upward.
Reflux that affects the throat and voice box, often without classic heartburn symptoms.
A condition where part of the stomach pushes upward through the diaphragm, increasing reflux risk.
A class of medications that reduce stomach acid production to help manage GERD symptoms.
A bundled Medicare plan (Part C) offered by private insurers, combining hospital, medical, and often drug coverage with extra benefits.
The list of prescription drugs covered by a Medicare Advantage or Part D plan. Each plan has its own formulary and drug tiers.
The highest amount you’ll pay in a year for covered services under a Medicare Advantage plan. Original Medicare does not have this cap.
A requirement that your doctor or plan must get approval before a service is covered. Common in Medicare Advantage plans.
Extra services offered by Medicare Advantage plans, such as dental, vision, hearing, fitness, transportation, and OTC allowances.
A type of Medicare Advantage plan that allows you to use doctors and hospitals outside the plan’s network, often at a higher cost.
A type of Medicare Advantage plan that requires you to use doctors and hospitals within the plan’s network for coverage.
The annual window (October 15 – December 7) when you can change Medicare Advantage or Part D plans.
The January 1 – March 31 window when you can switch Medicare Advantage plans or return to Original Medicare.
A naturally occurring compound that helps muscles recycle quick-burst energy for strength, balance, and daily movement.
The primary energy molecule used by cells for movement, muscle contraction, and metabolic activity.
A stored form of quick-release energy in muscle cells that powers short, functional movements like standing or climbing stairs.
The system that governs how muscles create, store, and use energy for strength, mobility, and daily function.
A metabolite of leucine that helps reduce muscle breakdown, especially during inactivity or illness.
The minimum amount of leucine needed in a meal to trigger muscle protein synthesis, typically around 2.5–3 grams.
The reduced ability of aging muscle to respond to protein, requiring higher and more targeted protein doses to stimulate growth.
Type II muscle fibers responsible for power, balance, and quick movements; they decline earlier and faster with age.
A cellular growth switch that increases protein synthesis when activated by amino acids, especially leucine.
The cell’s cleanup and recycling system that removes damaged proteins and mitochondria, supporting longevity and metabolic health.
A condition where the eyes do not produce enough tears or the tears evaporate too quickly, causing discomfort and blurry vision.
High-energy visible light emitted by screens and sunlight that can contribute to eye strain and visual fatigue.
The central part of the retina responsible for sharp, detailed vision needed for reading and recognizing faces.
The light-sensitive tissue at the back of the eye that converts images into signals sent to the brain.
A carotenoid antioxidant found in leafy greens that helps protect the retina from light damage.
A carotenoid that works with lutein to support macular health and visual clarity.
A simple habit to reduce screen strain: every 20 minutes, look at something 20 feet away for 20 seconds.
A thin layer of moisture that protects the eye surface and keeps vision clear.
Shielding the eyes from ultraviolet light to reduce the risk of cataracts and retinal damage.
An AI-powered tool that 'listens' to a consultation to generate medical notes, allowing the doctor to focus on the patient rather than a screen.
Often called 'Medical Glue' (like VenaSeal), this is a non-thermal 2026 standard for sealing varicose veins without the use of heat or multiple injections.
The medical term for a condition where the leg veins don't allow blood to flow back up to the heart effectively, often leading to varicose veins.
A diagnostic test that uses high-frequency sound waves to visualize the structure of the veins and the speed/direction of blood flow.
A procedure that uses a tiny laser fiber to deliver heat (thermal energy) to the vein wall, causing it to collapse and eventually be absorbed by the body.
A minimally invasive procedure where a doctor removes varicose veins through tiny 'micro-incisions' that typically do not require stitches.
In 2026, the new security standard used by health clinics to protect your vascular records from being decrypted by future advanced computers.
The clinical term for blood flowing in the wrong direction (backward) because of a failed valve.
Smaller, red or purple veins that sit closer to the skin’s surface. While often cosmetic, they can be a 'warning light' for deeper venous issues.
A specialized local anesthetic used in older thermal procedures (like EVLT) that requires multiple needle sticks around the target vein.
Age-related changes such as occasional forgetfulness or slower recall that do not significantly interfere with daily life.
A progressive decline in memory, thinking, and daily functioning that is not a normal part of aging.
A stage between normal aging and dementia with noticeable cognitive changes but mostly preserved independence.
The most common cause of dementia, often beginning with Mild Cognitive Impairment and progressing over time.
An FDA-approved monoclonal antibody that reduces amyloid plaques and slows early Alzheimer’s disease progression.
An investigational monoclonal antibody that targets amyloid plaques and has shown benefit in early Alzheimer’s disease trials.
Abnormal protein clumps in the brain associated with Alzheimer’s disease and targeted by drugs like lecanemab and donanemab.
A yearly Medicare visit that can include questions or screening for memory and cognitive changes.
The process where skeletal muscle compensates for impaired liver detoxification by removing ammonia from the bloodstream.
A metabolic imbalance in cirrhosis where branched-chain amino acids are depleted due to increased ammonia detoxification demands.
The nighttime metabolic pattern in cirrhosis where the body rapidly breaks down muscle due to limited glycogen stores.
A disruption in how the body allocates carbohydrates, fats, and proteins, common in advanced liver disease.
Muscle wasting caused by chronic inflammation, altered amino acid balance, and impaired liver function.

A decline in brain function that occurs when a damaged liver cannot properly filter toxins (like ammonia) from the blood, allowing them to reach the brain. Symptoms range from mild 'brain fog' to severe confusion or motor issues.

A condition where the inner lining of the blood vessels (the endothelium) fails to perform its normal functions, such as regulating blood pressure and preventing clots. In chronic inflammatory states like psoriasis, this is often the first step toward heart disease.

A measurement that evaluates the impact of carbohydrate consumption by taking into account both the Glycemic Index (how quickly sugar rises) and the total amount of carbohydrates in a typical serving. This offers a more practical understanding of a food's effect on blood sugar than the Glycemic Index alone.
